1: 'Blow a trumpet in Zion; sound an alarm on my holy mountain! Let all the inhabitants of the land tremble, for the day of the LORD is coming; it is near.' The shofar blown in Zion is the war alarm — the signal to gather the community for defense (cf. Numbers 10:9, Amos 3:6). The 'holy mountain' is Jerusalem/Zion itself (cf. Psalms 48:1). The second 'the day of the LORD is near' (first in Joel 1:15) — the locust invasion is being reinterpreted: it is not merely an agricultural disaster but a harbinger of the ultimate Day. The connection between local crisis and cosmic eschatology is characteristic of Joel's theological method.
2: 'A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness! Like blackness there is spread upon the mountains a great and powerful people; their like has never been before, nor will be again after them through the years of all generations.' 'Darkness and gloom, clouds and thick darkness' (yom choshek va'afelah yom anan va'araphel) are the classic theophany terms: Sinai (Exodus 19:16, Joel 20:21), the Day of YHWH in Zephaniah 1:15 ('a day of darkness and gloom'), and the NT's description of the crucifixion darkness (Matthew 27:45). The 'great and powerful people' is the locust army. 'Their like has never been before' — the same formula as the Egyptian plagues (Exodus 10:14: 'there had never been such locusts').
3: 'Fire devours before them, and behind them a flame burns. The land is like the garden of Eden before them, but behind them a desolate wilderness, and nothing escapes them.' Before the locust swarm: a garden. Behind them: wilderness. This is an exact description of locust devastation — aerial photographs of locust swarms show the precise boundary between stripped and unstripped vegetation. 'The garden of Eden' before, 'a desolate wilderness' behind — the reversal of creation's abundance to pre-creation chaos. The Eden reference establishes that this is not just agricultural loss but a cosmological reversal.
4: 'Their appearance is like the appearance of horses, and like war horses they run.' The comparison of locusts to horses is cross-cultural: the Italian word for locust, cavalletta (little horse), reflects the same visual observation. The locust's head actually resembles a small horse — this is so consistent across cultures that it is preserved in Revelation 9:7 ('the locusts looked like horses prepared for battle'). The 'war horse' image introduces the military metaphor that dominates vv4-9, making the agricultural disaster into a cosmic military campaign.
5: 'As with the rumbling of chariots, they leap on the tops of the mountains, like the crackling of a flame of fire devouring the stubble, like a powerful army drawn up for battle.' Three comparisons: (1) chariots rumbling — the sound of a locust swarm is indeed like thunder or distant wheels; (2) fire crackling over stubble — the dry crackle of locust movement through grain; (3) an army in battle array (am atzum meurak milchamah). The triple comparison builds the military metaphor: sound, appearance, and organization all correspond to an army. Ancient armies and locusts were both dreaded phenomena beyond individual control.
6: 'Before them peoples are in anguish; all faces grow pale.' The fear response to the locust army: peoples in anguish (yachilu, writhe in birth-pangs) and faces 'gathering blackness' (kol-panim qibetzu fharur) — the Hebrew idiom for the pallor of terror. The same face-blackening appears in Nahum 2:10 (Nineveh's fall) — a physiological description of fear-induced pallor. The scope has expanded: it is no longer just Israel that trembles before this army, but 'peoples' (ammim) — the locust plague/Day of YHWH has universal scope.
7: 'They run like mighty warriors; they climb the wall like soldiers; they march each on his way; they do not swerve from their paths.' The disciplined military advance of the locust swarm is a well-observed phenomenon: locust swarms do not deviate from their direction of advance; individual locusts maintain their course even while climbing vertical surfaces. 'They do not swerve from their paths' (velo ye'abetun oruchotam) — the same verb (avar) used of armies that do not deviate from their assigned lines. ANE military precision was the ideal; the locusts surpass even that ideal.
8: 'They do not jostle one another; each marches in his path; they burst through the weapons and are not halted.' Within the swarm, individual locusts do not interfere with each other — they move through narrow spaces without breaking formation. 'They burst through the weapons' (uvaad hashelach yipolu velo yivtzeru) — even weapons cannot stop them. This is the locust plague's terrifying quality from a military perspective: there is no defense against it. The locust army is invincible — which is the theological point: this is YHWH's army (v11), and no earthly defense can resist YHWH's judgment.
9: 'They leap upon the city, they run upon the walls, they climb up into the houses, they enter through the windows like a thief.' The total penetration of the city: locusts climb walls, enter buildings through any opening. Contemporaneous accounts of locust invasions describe exactly this behavior — locusts entering houses, filling rooms, impossible to exclude. The 'thief through the window' image appears in Jeremiah 9:21 for death entering through windows. The city that is supposed to provide security against enemies provides none against this divinely-sent army.
10: 'The earth quakes before them; the heavens tremble. The sun and the moon are darkened, and the stars withdraw their shining.' The cosmic response to the locust army — the sun darkened as a large swarm passes (historically observed; a dense swarm can reduce sunlight noticeably), but now expanded to cosmic theophany. 'Earth quakes, heavens tremble' are standard theophanic language (Psalms 68:8, Judges 5:4-5, Habakkuk 3:6). The darkening of sun, moon, and stars will recur in Joel 3:15 and is the signature cosmic sign of the Day of YHWH — cited in Matthew 24:29, Mark 13:24-25, and Revelation 6:12.
11: 'The LORD utters his voice before his army, for his camp is exceedingly great; he who executes his word is powerful. For the day of the LORD is great and very awesome; who can endure it?' YHWH 'utters his voice' (YHWH natan qolo) — thunder before his army, as in Ps 29 (the thunder-theophany) and Psalms 46:6. The army of locusts is explicitly identified as YHWH's army (machaneihu, his camp). The rhetorical question 'who can endure it?' (mi yechilenu) — who can sustain the weight of the Day of YHWH? — finds its NT echo in Revelation 6:17: 'the great day of their wrath has come, and who can stand?'
12: 'Yet even now, declares the LORD, return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning.' The divine 'yet even now' (vegam-attah) — even in the midst of judgment, even at this late hour, repentance remains possible. This is the defining characteristic of Hosea's and Joel's prophetic theology: judgment is not absolute fate but a call to return. 'With all your heart' echoes Deuteronomy 6:5 (the Shema) and Deuteronomy 30:10 (the call to return). The three outward expressions of repentance — fasting (tzom), weeping (bechi), mourning (misped) — are the physical forms of the internal turn.
13: 'And rend your hearts and not your garments. Return to the LORD your God, for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love; and he relents over disaster.' The call to 'rend your hearts and not your garments' is one of the OT's most precise distinctions between external religious form and internal reality. Tearing garments was the standard mourning gesture (Genesis 37:34, 2 Samuel 1:11, Joel 3:31, Ezra 9:3); Joel demands the tearing be internal. The divine character description — 'gracious and merciful, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love' — is the Exodus 34:6-7 formula (YHWH's own self-description to Moses), foundational to Israel's covenant theology and cited in Jonah 4:2, Psalms 86:15, Joel 103:8, Joel 145:8, Nehemiah 9:17.
14: 'Who knows whether he will not turn and relent, and leave a blessing behind him, a grain offering and a drink offering for the LORD your God?' The 'who knows' (mi yodea) is the language of humble uncertainty about divine response — the same phrase used by the king of Nineveh in Jonah 3:9: 'who knows whether God will not turn and relent?' This parallel between Joel 2:14 and Jonah 3:9 suggests either contemporaneity or literary dependence. The 'blessing' left behind is specifically agricultural — grain offering and drink offering, the same offerings cut off in Joel 1:9. Repentance may restore what the locust took.
15: 'Blow the trumpet in Zion; consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly.' A second shofar-blast in Zion — the first (v1) was for alarm; this one is for assembly. The sequence mirrors the Mosaic instructions for trumpet-blowing in Numbers 10:1-10: one blast for assembly, two for alarm. The solemn assembly (atzarah) was the technical term for the closing assembly of major festivals (Leviticus 23:36, Numbers 29:35, Deuteronomy 16:8) — now repurposed for emergency fasting. The progression from alarm (v1) to assembly (v15) marks the movement from crisis to response.
16: 'Gather the people. Consecrate the congregation; assemble the elders; gather the children, even nursing infants. Let the bridegroom leave his room, and the bride her chamber.' The unprecedented totality of the assembly: 'children, even nursing infants' (olelim veyone'key shadayim) — even those too young to participate consciously are included in the community's posture of repentance. 'The bridegroom leave his room' — in normal circumstances the new bridegroom was exempt from military duty for one year (Deuteronomy 24:5) and presumably from other communal obligations; the crisis suspends even the exemption for the newly married. The bridal chamber represents the innermost private sphere; even that privacy is broken open by this emergency.
17: 'Between the vestibule and the altar let the priests, the ministers of the LORD, weep and say, Spare your people, O LORD, and make not your heritage a reproach, a byword among the nations. Why should they say among the peoples, Where is their God?' The specific liturgical location: between the porch (ulam) and the altar (mizbei'ach) — this is the inner court of the Jerusalem temple, the space immediately before the altar of burnt offering. The prayer content invokes two covenant concerns: (1) YHWH's name/reputation among the nations ('Where is their God?' was the taunt of the nations when Israel suffered, cf. Psalms 79:10, Joel 115:2); (2) the status of Israel as YHWH's 'heritage' (nachalah), the land and people given as inheritance.
18: 'Then the LORD became jealous for his land and had pity on his people.' The pivot verse — divine response to the prayer of vv15-17. YHWH's 'jealousy' (yiqana) for his land is covenantal: the same 'jealousy' that drove him to judgment (Joel 2:18) is now turned toward restoration. 'Pity' (yachmol) — the root chamal means to spare through compassion. The land itself is the object of divine jealousy, not merely the people — consistent with the Deuteronomic covenant in which the land participates in the relationship between YHWH and Israel.
19: 'The LORD answered and said to his people, Behold, I am sending to you grain, wine, and oil, and you will be satisfied; and I will no more make you a reproach among the nations.' The divine answer is precisely the agricultural reversal of Joel 1: grain, wine, and oil — the three staple crops destroyed — are restored. 'I will no more make you a reproach' (velo-etten etchem od cherpah vagoyim) — the reproach of the nations (the 'where is their God?' taunt of v17) is answered by visible blessing. The covenant pattern: judgment brings reproach; restoration brings honor.
20: 'I will remove the northerner far from you, and drive him into a parched and desolate land, his vanguard into the eastern sea, and his rear guard into the western sea; its stench and foul smell will rise, for he has done great things.' The 'northerner' (hatzefoni) is the locust army — typically locusts come from the north in the Palestinian context, or the term may reflect ANE tradition of disaster coming from the north (cf. Jeremiah 1:14, Joel 4:6, Joel 6:1 — 'out of the north evil comes'). 'Eastern sea' = the Dead Sea; 'western sea' = the Mediterranean. The locust horde driven into both seas and rotting — historically, when locust swarms reach the sea they drown and wash up in vast putrefying masses.
21: 'Fear not, O land; be glad and rejoice, for the LORD has done great things!' The reversal call: the land personified (as it mourned in Joel 1:10) is now called to rejoice (gili vesimchi). 'The LORD has done great things' (higdil YHWH la'asot) — the phrase echoes Psalms 126:3 ('The LORD has done great things for us; we are glad'). The direct address to the land reflects the OT's view that the covenant affects the non-human creation — the land mourns under judgment and rejoices under blessing (cf. Psalms 96:11-13, Joel 98:7-9).
22: 'Fear not, you beasts of the field, for the pastures of the wilderness are green; the tree bears its fruit; the fig tree and vine give their full yield.' The beasts of the field — who groaned in 1:18 and panted in Joel 1:20 — are addressed directly. The restoration is complete: pastures green, fig and vine yielding fully. The sequence from Joel 1 to Joel 2:22 forms a chiastic structure: (1) locust destroys vine and fig (Joel 1:7), (2) trees wither (Joel 1:12), (3) beasts groan (Joel 1:18), (4) animals pant for water (Joel 1:20); now reversed: (4) animals comforted (Joel 2:22), (3) pastures green (Joel 2:22), (2) fig and vine yield (Joel 2:22), (1) the full agricultural restoration follows in vv23-24.
23: 'Be glad, O children of Zion, and rejoice in the LORD your God, for he has given the early rain for your vindication; he has poured down for you abundant rain, the early and the latter rain, as before.' The 'early rain' (moreh litzedaqah) — rain of righteousness/vindication — is the autumn rain that softens the soil for plowing (October-November in Palestine). The 'latter rain' (malqosh) is the spring rain (March-April) that fills out the grain before harvest. The two rainy seasons defined Palestinian agriculture; their regularity was a sign of covenant blessing (Deuteronomy 11:14). The rain as 'for your vindication' (litzedaqah) suggests that the restoration of rain demonstrates YHWH's righteousness in restoring his people.
24: 'The threshing floors shall be full of grain; the vats shall overflow with wine and oil.' The threshing floor (goren) — the paved hilltop platform where grain was threshed by oxen or a sledge — full of grain again, the vats flowing with wine and oil. This is the exact reversal of Joel 1:17 (storehouses desolate, granaries torn down). The fullness of the threshing floor and overflowing vats are the material expression of covenant blessing — the land is productive again, the covenant relationship restored.
25: 'I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten, the hopper, the destroyer, and the cutter, my great army, which I sent among you.' YHWH identifies the locusts explicitly as 'my great army, which I sent among you' (cheyeli haggadol asher shillachti bachem). The locust plague was not a natural disaster in Joel's theology but a divine instrument. The promise to 'restore the years' (shillamti lachem et-hashenim) is remarkable: not merely to provide a good harvest next year, but to compensate for all that was lost. The four locust terms from 1:4 reappear in reverse order, emphasizing that this is a complete reversal.
26: 'You shall eat in plenty and be satisfied, and praise the name of the LORD your God, who has dealt wondrously with you. And my people shall never again be put to shame.' The covenant pattern of eating, satisfaction, and praise mirrors Deuteronomy 8:10-11: 'when you have eaten and are satisfied, praise the LORD your God.' The key prohibition in Deuteronomy 8:11 is 'do not forget' — here the promise is positive: eating leads to praising the name of YHWH. 'Never again be put to shame' (velo-yevoshu ammi le'olam) — the reproach that Israel suffered (v17, 19) is permanently removed. This promise becomes the background for Paul's 'whoever believes in him will not be put to shame' (Romans 10:11, quoting Isaiah 28:16).
27: 'You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am the LORD your God and there is none else. And my people shall never again be put to shame.' The covenant knowledge formula — 'you shall know that I am the LORD' (yeda'tem ki qirbi Yisrael) — combined with the monotheistic confession 'there is none else' (ve'ein od). This is the Deutero-Isaiah language (Isaiah 44:6, Joel 45:5, 6, 14, 18, 21, 22) applied to Joel's restoration. YHWH's presence 'in the midst of Israel' (bequirbi Yisrael) is the covenant's central promise — the divine presence in the Tabernacle/Temple that defines Israel as a covenant people.
28: 'And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions.' The great eschatological prophecy: ve'acharei-ken (after this/afterward) marks the transition from historical restoration to eschatological fulfillment. 'Pour out my Spirit on all flesh' (ashpoch et-ruchi al-kol-basar) — 'all flesh' (kol-basar) breaks all previous limits on prophetic experience: gender (sons AND daughters), age (old AND young), and social status (v29: servants AND handmaids). This democratization of the Spirit is the most radical promise in Joel and one of the most radical in the OT. Peter quotes it verbatim in Acts 2:17-21 as the interpretation of Pentecost.
29: 'Even on the male and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit.' The explicit inclusion of servants (avadim) and female servants (shefachot) is remarkable: these were the lowest social class in ancient Israel, with minimal legal standing. That the Spirit would be poured on them equally with the sons of the house radical. The legal category of slavery is not abolished here but spiritually transcended: all flesh, regardless of social standing, receives the Spirit equally. This egalitarian dimension of Joel's prophecy becomes central to Paul's theology ('there is neither slave nor free... in Christ Jesus,' Galatians 3:28).
30: 'And I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth, blood and fire and columns of smoke.' The cosmic signs accompanying the Day of YHWH: blood (dam), fire (esh), and columns of smoke (timrot ashan). 'Columns of smoke' — the Hebrew timarot is related to the palm-tree (tamar) shape of a vertical smoke column. These signs have both immediate historical reference (volcanic or military events) and eschatological dimension. Peter quotes vv30-31 in Acts 2:19-20 as being fulfilled at Pentecost, understanding the cosmic signs as pointing to the significance of the Christ-event.
31: 'The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes.' The astronomical portents: sun to darkness, moon to blood. 'Moon to blood' (hayareach ledam) — a lunar eclipse turns the moon copper-red; such eclipses were interpreted as omens in the ancient world. In the NT, this verse is quoted in Acts 2:20 (applied to Pentecost), Revelation 6:12 (the sixth seal), and alluded to in Mark 13:24-25/Matthew 24:29. The 'great and awesome day of the LORD' (yom YHWH haggadol vehanora) is the climactic event toward which all of Joel points — the final intervention of YHWH in history.
32: 'And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved. For in Mount Zion and in Jerusalem there shall be those who escape, as the LORD has said, and among the survivors shall be those whom the LORD calls.' 'Everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved' (vehayah kol asher-yiqra beshem YHWH yimalet) — the universal promise of salvation through the divine name. Paul quotes this in Romans 10:13 in the context of salvation through confessing Jesus as Lord (connecting with the divine-name theme in Philippians 2:9-11). Acts 2:21 quotes it as fulfilled at Pentecost. The 'survivors' (serida) and those whom 'the LORD calls' introduce the election motif: both calling on the name and being called by YHWH are present.
